article_title: Microsoft Ties Mastra npm Supply-Chain Attack to North Korea's Sapphire Sleet as 'easy-day-js' Typosquat Poisons 140-Plus AI Packages
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: Microsoft Ties Mastra npm Supply-Chain Attack to North Korea's Sapphire Sleet as 'easy-day-js' Typosquat Poisons 140-Plus AI Packages
content_preview.summary: Microsoft attributes the June 17 compromise of 140-plus Mastra npm packages to North Korean state actor Sapphire Sleet, which used a hijacked maintainer account to inject a malicious dayjs typosquat called easy-day-js.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

More than 140 npm packages belonging to Mastra, a widely used open-source JavaScript and TypeScript framework for building AI applications, were compromised on June 17, 2026, in an automated software supply-chain attack. According to the [Microsoft Security Blog](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2026/06/17/postinstall-payload-inside-mastra-npm-supply-chain-compromise/), "Microsoft Threat Intelligence observed a large-scale npm supply chain attack affecting 140+ packages...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (791 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Clear Overview / What We Know / The Scale / What We Don't Know / Analysis sections. Reporting is descriptive and stays at the incident-reporting level: it does not reproduce or expand attack code, payload internals, or step-by-step exploitation mechanics, which is appropriate for a disclosed security incident.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 6 source snapshots read from disk (all fetched status_code 200). source-0.html.gz (Microsoft Security Blog): CONFIRMED all four attributed quotes verbatim — '140+ packages across the mastra and @mastra scopes', 'with high confidence that this activity is attributable to Sapphire Sleet', 'a North Korean state actor that primarily targets the financial sector', and the full 'ehindero npm maintainer account' takeover sentence. source-1.html.gz (JFrog Security Research): CONFIRMED 'This campaign includes 143 affected Mastra packages and a single malicious dependency, easy-day-js' verbatim; CONFIRMED the two-stage timeline — npm registry metadata records easy-day-js@1.11.21 at 2026-06-16 and easy-day-js@1.11.22 at 2026-06-17, matching the article's June 16 / June 17 dating and clean-then-weaponized framing. source-2.html.gz (StepSecurity): CONFIRMED 'Over the next 88 minutes, 140+ packages across the entire Mastra ecosystem were republished' and 'Packages with a combined weekly download count exceeding 1.1 million were exposed' verbatim; CONFIRMED impersonation details (author metadata iamkun, homepage, repository URL, MIT license, 1.11.x versioning). source-3.html.gz (Snyk): CONFIRMED 'signs of takeover rather than insider action, including an email change away from the contributor's original address' and '142 publishable packages across the @mastra/* scope plus mastra, create-mastra, and mastracode' verbatim; CONFIRMED @mastra/core ~4 million downloads/month and mastra ~1.5 million more, aggregate into the tens of millions. source-4.html.gz (RH-ISAC): CONFIRMED the 'ehindero' account hijack quote verbatim (curly quotes in source) and the 144-package figure and maintainer-phishing framing. source-5.html.gz (Sonatype): CONFIRMED 'a malicious package designed to impersonate the popular JavaScript date library dayjs', tracking id sonatype-2026-003926, and the postinstall / second-stage-payload description verbatim. Package-count figures cross-checked: 142 correctly attributed to Snyk, 143 to JFrog, 144 to RH-ISAC — each number maps to the outlet that reported it.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every specific (dates, version numbers, package counts, download figures, threat-actor name, account name, campaign id) traces to a cited source and matches the snapshot. No hallucinated quotes or fabricated specifics detected. The article correctly attributes the differing package counts to their respective outlets and correctly frames Microsoft's attribution as Microsoft Threat Intelligence's own assessment.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. Uses hedged, sourced language ('estimates vary', 'Microsoft assessed', 'as of the cited disclosures'). No sensationalism or editorializing despite the nation-state angle. The 'What We Don't Know' section responsibly enumerates the limits of public reporting.
editor_notes.originality: New topic. No prior Machine Herald coverage of the Mastra / easy-day-js / Sapphire Sleet incident; grep of the archive returned only unrelated hits (MIT-license and chemistry articles).
concerns: Two cited sources are not on config/source_allowlist.txt: research.jfrog.com (JFrog Security Research) and rhisac.org (RH-ISAC). Both are reputable security-industry sources and every claim attributed to them was verified verbatim against the committed snapshots, so this is an off-allowlist warning judged editorially acceptable rather than a factual defect. Documented in the corrections record per the automated recommendation.
recommendations: Maintainers may consider adding research.jfrog.com and rhisac.org to the source allowlist given their established security-research standing (allowlist not modified as part of this review).
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Accurate, well-sourced, neutral News report on a publicly disclosed npm supply-chain incident. All quotes verbatim and all specifics traced to cited snapshots. Approved with a corrections record filed to transparently note the two off-allowlist (but reputable and fully verified) sources.
